scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: Ms. ___ is a 43-year-old female with a history of Type 1 diabetes and gastroparesis who was admitted for diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) precipitated by a gastroparesis flare and insulin noncompliance. She was successfully treated with an insulin infusion and intravenous hydration, resulting in the closure of her anion gap and improvement of her acute-on-chronic kidney injury. Her gastroparesis symptoms were managed with antiemetics and her home nocturnal jejunal tube feeding regimen; however, her bedtime glargine was reduced to 14 units prior to discharge to prevent further episodes of hypoglycemia. The patient was also transitioned from omeprazole to lansoprazole to maintain GJ-tube patency and was educated on the critical importance of continuing basal insulin and maintaining glucose intake during illness. She was discharged in stable condition, independently ambulatory, with home health services arranged to support her diabetes management and tube care. No further evidence of infection was found, as her admission leukocytosis resolved with DKA treatment and repeat C. difficile testing was negative.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis:
The patient is a 43-year-old female with a chief complaint of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) due to poor oral intake and insulin noncompliance. She has a history of type 1 diabetes, complicated by retinopathy, neuropathy, nephropathy, and gastroparesis. She was recently discharged with DKA and was readmitted to the ICU for DKA management and insulin drip.

The patient's past medical history includes hypercholesterolemia, hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, fibromyalgia, degenerative joint disease, depression, status post appendectomy, multiple exploratory laparotomies for endometriosis, and hysterectomy. She has a history of pelvic inflammatory disease and has a narcotics contract with her primary care physician for hydromorphone.

The patient's allergies include Relafen and Leflunomide. She has a history of gastroparesis and was successfully treated for Clostridioides difficile (C. diff) with oral Flagyl two weeks prior to admission.

The patient's diagnosis includes DKA, diabetes mellitus type 1, leukocytosis, and abdominal pain likely due to her known gastroparesis. She was treated with insulin drip, hydration, and antiemetics, and her G-tube output remained unchanged.

The patient's medications at discharge include amlodipine, carvedilol, fluoxetine, rosuvastatin, lansoprazole, and lorazepam.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: Ms. ___ is a 54-year-old female with a history of peripheral vascular disease and a prior left femoral to posterior tibial bypass who was admitted for evaluation of worsening bilateral toe ulcers and rest pain. She underwent a diagnostic bilateral lower extremity angiogram which revealed extensive arterial disease, including occlusions of the bilateral superficial femoral and popliteal arteries and the previous bypass graft. The procedure was uncomplicated, and by the first postoperative day, she was clinically stable, afebrile, and ambulating with the assistance of a walker. Although she eloped from the hospital prior to formal discharge teaching, she was alert and cooperative with nursing for the removal of her intravenous line. She has been instructed to continue her home medications, excluding lisinopril and rivaroxaban which are held until her next procedure, and to maintain her existing podiatry wound care regimen with Adaptic and Kerlix. She is scheduled to return to the hospital on ___ for a planned redo left femoral to posterior tibial bypass.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis:
The patient is a 54-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital for evaluation of her peripheral vascular disease and toe ulcers. She has a history of left femoral to posterior tibial bypass graft completed in the past, and has been experiencing toe pain and difficulty with ambulation. The patient has a history of ovarian cancer, hypertension, and other medical conditions. She is allergic to penicillins and NSAIDs.

The patient underwent a diagnostic angiogram of her bilateral lower extremities, which showed occlusion of the left SFA, bypass graft, and popliteal artery, as well as occlusion of the right SFA and popliteal artery. She was admitted to the hospital for monitoring and was discharged home without services.

The patient's medications at admission included acetaminophen, lisinopril, gabapentin, mirtazapine, rivaroxaban, atorvastatin, oxyCODONE, and omeprazole. Her medications at discharge included acetaminophen, aspirin, atorvastatin, gabapentin, mirtazapine, omeprazole, and oxyCODONE, with lisinopril and rivaroxaban held until her planned readmission.

The patient's discharge diagnosis was peripheral vascular disease and toe ulcers, and her discharge condition was clear and coherent, with a level of consciousness of alert and interactive, and activity status of ambulatory with assistance or aid.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: This 18-year-old male with an unremarkable medical history was admitted after sustaining right mandibular body and ramus fractures from a baseball injury. He underwent surgical repair involving open reduction and internal fixation of the mandibular body and closed reduction with maxillo-mandibular fixation of the right sub-condylar fracture. His postoperative recovery was stable; he tolerated a transition to a full liquid diet and maintained good pain control on oral Hydromorphone with Docusate for bowel prophylaxis. Post-operative imaging confirmed stable interval fixation of his injuries, and he was discharged on postoperative day one in stable condition. The patient was instructed to maintain meticulous oral hygiene using saltwater and Chlorhexidine rinses and to manage swelling with alternating cold and warm compresses. He was also advised to avoid strenuous physical activity and contact sports until cleared during his follow-up appointment with the oral surgery department.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis:
The patient is an 18-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital after being hit by a line drive while playing baseball. He presented with jaw pain and was diagnosed with a nondisplaced right mandibular fracture. He underwent an open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) of the fracture, as well as closed reduction with maxillo-mandibular fixation of a right sub-condylar fracture. The patient's surgical pain was controlled with intravenous analgesia and later converted to oral agents. He was started on clears and advanced to a full liquid diet. The patient was discharged home on post-operative day (POD) #1 in stable condition.

The patient's past medical history is unremarkable, and he has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. His vital signs upon discharge were normal, with a temperature of 98.2°F, blood pressure of 143/62 mmHg, heart rate of 62 beats per minute, and respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute.

The patient was prescribed three medications at discharge: chlorhexidine gluconate oral rinse, docusate sodium, and hydromorphone (Dilaudid) for pain control. He was instructed to follow a wound care regimen, including avoiding disturbing the surgical area and using gauze to control bleeding. He was also advised to avoid strenuous physical activity, use ice applications to minimize swelling, and begin normal oral hygiene the day after surgery.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: Mrs. [Name] is a 74-year-old female with a history of severe aortic stenosis and coronary artery disease who was admitted for elective surgical management. She successfully underwent coronary artery bypass grafting to two vessels, aortic valve replacement with a 21mm porcine bioprosthesis, and closure of a patent foramen ovale. Her postoperative course was complicated by new-onset atrial fibrillation, managed with amiodarone and warfarin, and postoperative anemia requiring a transfusion of two units of packed red blood cells. She also experienced a period of transient confusion and leukocytosis that was treated empirically with a short course of ciprofloxacin and diuresis, with her mental status returning to baseline prior to discharge. Mrs. [Name] was discharged home in stable condition with visiting nurse and physical therapy services to assist with her recovery and wound monitoring. Her discharge plan includes strict sternal precautions, a 10-pound lifting restriction for ten weeks, and a medication regimen including metoprolol, aspirin, and atorvastatin, along with short-term courses of furosemide and potassium.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis:
The patient is a 74-year-old female who was admitted for surgical management of her aortic valve and coronary artery disease. She has a history of aortic stenosis, patent foramen ovale, coronary artery disease, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. She is allergic to erythromycin base. 

The patient underwent coronary artery bypass grafting to two vessels, aortic valve replacement with a 21mm porcine valve, and PFO closure. Postoperatively, she developed atrial fibrillation and was started on amiodarone and coumadin. She was also transfused with two units of packed red blood cells for postoperative anemia. 

The patient's medications at discharge include amiodarone, docusate sodium, aspirin, potassium chloride, atorvastatin, metoprolol tartrate, pantoprazole, furosemide, ciprofloxacin, and warfarin. She was discharged home with service and was advised to follow up with her cardiologist.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: The patient is a 22-year-old female who presented with a one-month history of right leg pain and significant leukocytosis, leading to a diagnosis of chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) confirmed by BCR/ABL fluorescence in situ hybridization and bone marrow biopsy. MRI of the right femur demonstrated marrow infiltration consistent with CML without evidence of fracture or lytic lesions, and her pain was successfully managed with a transition to acetaminophen prior to discharge. During her stay, she was initiated on dasatinib therapy and received allopurinol for tumor lysis syndrome prophylaxis. Her medication regimen was adjusted to stop omeprazole due to interactions with her leukemia treatment, and she was transitioned to a progesterone-only contraceptive to reduce the risk of thromboembolism. Significant anxiety related to her new diagnosis was addressed with psychiatric consultation and a referral for outpatient follow-up. She was discharged in stable condition, independently ambulatory with full weight-bearing status, and instructed to maintain close follow-up with hematology-oncology for ongoing management of her leukemia.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis: The patient is a 22-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of right thigh pain and elevated WBC count. She has no major past medical history, but has had a laparoscopic cholecystectomy and has been diagnosed with GERD. She has allergies to penicillins and amoxicillin. The patient's physical examination revealed normal vital signs, with the exception of a slightly elevated blood pressure. Laboratory results showed an abnormal complete blood count, with a WBC count of 139.3 and a platelet count of 123. Bone marrow biopsy revealed a translocation involving chromosomes 9 and 22, consistent with chronic myelogenous leukemia. The patient was diagnosed with chronic myelogenous leukemia and underwent a bone marrow aspirate and biopsy. She was prescribed pain control medication and was discharged from the hospital.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: This 59-year-old male with a history of alcohol-induced cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma was admitted for a deceased donor liver transplant. His postoperative course was complex, requiring a surgical re-exploration for suspected vascular obstruction, management of new-onset atrial fibrillation and flutter with digoxin, and a pericardial window for a large pericardial effusion. Additionally, he underwent an ERCP with biliary stenting for a bile leak and CT-guided placement of an abdominal pigtail catheter to manage a bilious collection. Despite experiencing transient postoperative delirium that resolved before discharge, the patient's condition stabilized, and he was alert, coherent, and independently ambulatory at the time of transition. He was discharged home with an immunosuppressive regimen of tacrolimus, mycophenolate, and a prednisone taper, alongside prophylactic medications and digoxin for rate control. Home health services were arranged to assist with his abdominal drain management, and he will continue close follow-up with the transplant clinic for frequent lab monitoring and further care.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis: The patient is a 59-year-old male who was admitted for a liver transplant due to HCC/ETOH cirrhosis. He has a past medical history of EtOH cirrhosis, skin cancer, and has undergone TIPS and RFA procedures. He has no known allergies or adverse reactions. The patient underwent a deceased donor liver transplant and was intubated post-operatively due to respiratory failure. He was later extubated and transferred to the floor for post-operative care. During his hospital stay, he developed atrial fibrillation, which was treated with digoxin and propranolol. He also developed a pericardial effusion, which was drained and treated with antibiotics. The patient's laboratory results showed normal liver function tests, and he was prescribed immunosuppressive medications, including Cellcept, myfortic, and tacrolimus. He was discharged with a drain in the mid abdominal collection and was advised to follow up with his healthcare provider for further treatment.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: This 79-year-old female with a history of hypertension, COPD, and a pacemaker was admitted following a motor vehicle accident after hitting a light pole, likely secondary to a syncopal event. An extensive trauma workup, including CT imaging of the head, neck, and torso, revealed no acute fractures or internal injuries; however, incidental 3-4 mm pulmonary nodules were identified, requiring a follow-up chest CT in 12 months. Evaluation of the syncope included a normal transthoracic echocardiogram and pacemaker interrogation with no significant events, but the patient was found to have symptomatic orthostatic hypotension. Consequently, she was treated with intravenous fluid boluses, and her home Verapamil dose was reduced to 240 mg daily. While an initial low morning cortisol level raised concern for adrenal insufficiency, a repeat test was normal, suggesting the initial value was likely influenced by prior fentanyl administration. The patient remained alert and independently ambulatory during her stay and was discharged to a rehabilitation facility to improve her stability, with strict instructions to refrain from driving and to follow up with her cardiologist regarding further blood pressure medication adjustments.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis: The patient is a 79-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital after a motor vehicle accident (MVA). She has a history of hypertension, hypothyroidism, and COPD. She was involved in a MVC, but does not remember the event. She complained of right-sided chest pain and diffuse back pain, and was found to have normal vital signs and laboratory results. She was diagnosed with a mild back strain and was prescribed pain medication. She also has a history of asthma/COPD, GERD, and a pacemaker for tachy/brady arrhythmia. She has no known allergies or adverse reactions. The patient's physical examination was normal, with the exception of some minor abrasions on her knees. She was discharged with a diagnosis of a mild back strain and was prescribed pain medication.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: This 49-year-old male with a history of hypertension, asthma, and diabetes was admitted for acute throat swelling and respiratory distress diagnosed as lisinopril-induced angioedema. Following stabilization with racemic epinephrine, corticosteroids, and antihistamines, fiberoptic laryngoscopy confirmed resolving hypopharyngeal and epiglottal edema during his observation in the intensive care unit. His hospital course also addressed a concurrent asthma flare with nebulized albuterol. At the time of discharge, the patient's airway was stable, his lungs were clear, and he was tolerating oral intake well. He was instructed to permanently avoid all ACE inhibitors and was discharged on his home medications along with a four-day course of prednisone, with instructions to follow up with his primary care physician for alternative blood pressure management and an allergy consultation.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis: The patient is a 49-year-old male who presented to the hospital with a chief complaint of throat swelling. He has a history of asthma, diabetes, and hypertension. He was taking lisinopril for hypertension, but it was discontinued during his hospital stay due to concerns about its potential contribution to his angioedema. The patient's symptoms included a sensation of throat swelling, rhinorrhea, and a cough productive of whitish sputum. He was treated with steroids, benadryl, and famotidine, and his angioedema resolved. He was also diagnosed with asthma and treated with albuterol nebulizers as needed. The patient was discharged home with a diagnosis of angioedema and asthma, and was prescribed medications including diphenhydramine, famotidine, prednisone, albuterol inhaler, fluticasone propionate, and metformin.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: This 43-year-old male with a history of chronic pancreatitis, SMV thrombosis, and insulin-dependent diabetes was admitted with a right upper quadrant abdominal wall mass and a loculated left pleural empyema, complicated by a supratherapeutic INR of 22. During his stay, he underwent a VATS decortication and surgical debridement of his abdominal wall abscess, with cultures from both sites identifying Fusobacterium; he was also treated for Clostridioides difficile. He was discharged home in stable condition with a PICC line to complete a four-week course of intravenous penicillin G and two weeks of oral metronidazole. His discharge plan includes daily visiting nursing for INR monitoring and Lovenox bridging until therapeutic on warfarin, as well as wound vacuum dressing changes every three days. He is tolerating a regular diet with pancreatic enzyme supplementation and is managing his diabetes with a home insulin regimen and his pain with an intensified oral narcotic schedule.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis:
The patient is a 43-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of a RUQ mass and pain, as well as a L pleural loculated fluid collection. He has a history of EtOH-induced chronic pancreatitis, SMV thrombosis, and is on Coumadin indefinitely. He also has chronic abdominal pain and is taking Oxycontin and Percocet. The patient's past medical history includes IDDM, chronic abdominal pain, chronic pancreatitis, SMV thrombosis, insomnia, and h/o alcoholism.

The patient's diagnosis includes a complex multiloculated cystic lesion in the right upper quadrant, possibly an abscess or hematoma, and a large loculated left pleural fluid collection. He also has sequela of chronic pancreatitis.

The patient underwent a tube thoracostomy (pigtail) and incision and drainage of the RUQ mass. He was prescribed medications at discharge, including antibiotics and pain medication.

The patient's physical exam revealed a thin male with a firm, extremely tender, erythematous mass deep to the RUQ scar. He had moderate TTP to palpation in the L hemi-abdomen, though he reported this was baseline. His vital signs were stable, and he was afebrile.

The patient's laboratory results showed an elevated white blood cell count, platelet count, and liver enzymes, consistent with an inflammatory process. His imaging studies, including CT and MRI, showed a complex multiloculated cystic lesion in the right upper quadrant, a large loculated left pleural fluid collection, and atrophy and calcification of the pancreas.

Overall, the patient's hospital course was marked by a complex and challenging presentation, requiring multiple procedures and a multidisciplinary approach to management.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: This 67-year-old male with end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis, type 2 diabetes, and coronary artery disease was initially admitted for chest pain and a cough concerning for tuberculosis. While myocardial infarction and active tuberculosis were successfully ruled out, his hospital course was complicated by an acute right MCA ischemic stroke occurring during hemodialysis. He received prompt tPA administration with significant improvement in his initial flaccid left-sided weakness, though he was discharged with a persistent left facial droop and mild expressive aphasia. His management focused on secondary stroke prevention through the initiation of high-dose atorvastatin, an increased aspirin dose of 325 mg, and optimized blood pressure control with carvedilol and lisinopril. Due to poor oral intake, Megace was started as an appetite stimulant, and tramadol was provided for his chronic headaches. He was discharged home in stable condition with instructions to use a walker for mobility and to engage in outpatient physical, occupational, and speech therapy, with plans for an outpatient transesophageal echocardiogram to further evaluate the stroke etiology.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis:
The patient is a 67-year-old male with a history of End Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) on hemodialysis, Type II Diabetes Mellitus, Coronary Artery Disease (CAD) with a drug-eluting stent, Hyperlipidemia, Hypertension, and Anemia. He was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of chest pain, tuberculosis rule-out, acute cerebral infarct, headache, and pulmonary edema. 

The patient's past medical history includes latent tuberculosis treated with INH, chronic hypertension, and chronic hemodialysis. His social history is significant for a sister who died from diabetes complications and multiple living children with various health problems. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with a right MCA perfusion defect with "penumbra" and was treated with tPA. He was also found to have a R carotid stenosis. The patient remains aphasic and has left upper extremity weakness, but is able to move his extremities and ambulate to the bathroom without assistance. 

At discharge, the patient's vital signs were stable, and he was prescribed medications for his conditions. His laboratory results showed normal blood chemistry values, except for slightly elevated glucose levels.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: Mr. ___ is a 70-year-old male with a history of hypertension and dyslipidemia who was admitted for substernal chest pain and diaphoresis, ultimately diagnosed with an NSTEMI with a peak troponin of 0.16. He underwent successful cardiac catheterization via right radial access with the placement of two drug-eluting stents to the mid and distal left anterior descending artery. His hospital course was also significant for a new diagnosis of diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 6.5%), for which he was started on a metformin titration alongside a cardiac regimen of aspirin, ticagrelor, metoprolol succinate, and high-dose atorvastatin. He was discharged home in stable condition with a stable radial access site and instructions to follow up with cardiology for stent management and his primary care physician for formal diabetic education and blood pressure monitoring.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis:
The patient is a 70-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital with chest pain. He has a history of hypertension, dyslipidemia, pre-diabetes, hemorrhoids/anal fissure, and benign prostatic hypertrophy. He presented with substernal chest pain that started in the morning while he was lying in bed, which was associated with sweating, feeling hot and clammy, and diaphoresis. The pain lasted for about an hour and was described as severe. He denied radiation of pain, numbness/tingling in his arms, shortness of breath, and abdominal/back pain.

The patient has a past medical history of cardiac risk factors, including pre-diabetes, hypertension, and dyslipidemia. He also has a history of dermatoheliosis, benign prostatic hypertrophy, inflammatory colon polyp, chronic sun-damaged skin, and seborrheic keratoses.

The patient's physical examination on admission showed a well-developed male in no acute distress, with a normal cardiovascular examination. His laboratory results showed elevated troponin levels, with a peak level of 0.16 ng/mL. He was started on heparin gtt and aspirin.

The patient underwent a left heart catheterization, which showed 90% occlusion in the mid and distal left anterior descending (LAD) artery, and two drug-eluting stents were placed with good revascularization.

The patient's diagnosis is mild regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction, consistent with coronary artery disease. He was prescribed medications at discharge, including aspirin, clopidogrel, and metoprolol.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: This 31-year-old male with a history of medically refractory chronic ulcerative colitis was admitted for a total abdominal colectomy and end ileostomy. The surgical procedure was successful, and pathology confirmed chronic active colitis without evidence of malignancy. His postoperative recovery was notable for transient right leg weakness that resolved completely after the epidural was discontinued, as well as non-ischemic shoulder pain that was managed with analgesics. He met all milestones for discharge, including tolerating a regular diet, achieving independent ambulation, and demonstrating stable ostomy output. The patient was discharged home in stable condition with instructions to monitor his ileostomy output closely for signs of dehydration and to maintain hydration with electrolyte-rich fluids. His pain management plan includes scheduled acetaminophen and as-needed oxycodone. He will receive home-based support from a visiting nurse for ostomy management and has been instructed to follow up in the clinic for staple removal and further ostomy teaching. He was advised to avoid heavy lifting for six weeks and to monitor his incision for any signs of infection.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis: The patient is a 31-year-old male who was admitted for a total abdominal colectomy with ileoanal pouch or end ileostomy due to chronic ulcerative colitis, which was medically refractory. The patient has a history of C. Diff colitis, ulcerative colitis, appendectomy, and right hand/finger debridement. He has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. The patient underwent the procedure well and was discharged with good pain control. At discharge, the patient was tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding well, and pain was well controlled. The patient was prescribed acetaminophen and oxycodone for pain management. He was also instructed to monitor his ileostomy output and to seek medical attention if he experiences dehydration symptoms.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: Mr. ___ is a 67-year-old male with a history of depression and gout who was hospitalized for worsening epigastric and right upper quadrant abdominal pain. Initial evaluation was significant for leukocytosis, transaminitis, and an elevated lipase, with imaging confirming mild pancreatitis and common bile duct dilation. He was diagnosed with acute cholecystitis and, after initial stabilization with intravenous fluids and antibiotics, underwent an uncomplicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy on hospital day three. His postoperative course was smooth; a minor gout flare was managed by continuing his home allopurinol and adding as-needed colchicine, and he successfully advanced to a regular diet. At the time of discharge, he was in stable condition, ambulating independently with pain well-controlled on a regimen of acetaminophen and oxycodone. He was instructed to follow up in the general surgery clinic in one to two weeks and to adhere to a lifting restriction of no more than ten pounds for six weeks.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis: The patient is a 67-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of abdominal pain. He has a past medical history of depression, gout, and a suicide attempt. He was transferred from another hospital with worsening abdominal pain and concern for cholangitis. The patient underwent a laparoscopic cholecystectomy and was diagnosed with cholecystitis. He was started on IV fluids, antibiotics, and bowel rest, and his diet was gradually advanced to regular. He was discharged home on post-operative day 1 in stable condition, with instructions to follow up in the general surgery clinic in several weeks. The patient was prescribed medications for pain, constipation, and gout, and was advised to take acetaminophen as needed for pain.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: This 58-year-old male with muscle-invasive urothelial carcinoma underwent a robot-assisted laparoscopic radical cystoprostatectomy, bilateral extended pelvic lymphadenectomy, and ileal conduit urinary diversion following neoadjuvant chemotherapy. His postoperative course was complicated by tachycardia and fluid positivity on day one, which resolved with metoprolol and gentle diuresis, as well as transient elevations in white blood cell count and creatine kinase that were monitored without further intervention. The patient successfully transitioned from patient-controlled analgesia to a multimodal oral pain regimen consisting of gabapentin, ibuprofen, and as-needed hydrocodone-acetaminophen. He was discharged home in stable condition with visiting nursing services for urostomy care, maintaining two ureteral stents intended for natural auto-ejection. He was instructed to follow a ten-pound lifting restriction for four weeks, utilize a "log-roll" technique for mobility, and remove the outer dressing of his JP drain site two days after discharge while leaving the underlying steri-strips intact. At the time of discharge, his urostomy was pink and patent, and his incisions were healing well without evidence of infection.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis:
The patient is a 58-year-old male who was admitted to the hospital for a robot-assisted laparoscopic radical cystoprostatectomy with bilateral extended pelvic lymphadenectomy and ileal conduit urinary diversion due to bladder cancer. His past medical history includes renal insufficiency, tendonitis, schistosomiasis, IBS, prostatitis, HLD, nephrolithiasis, and epididymitis. He has allergies to adhesive tape and Dicloxacillin.

The patient's presenting symptoms and reason for admission were related to his bladder cancer and the surgical procedure. He had a major surgical procedure performed, which included a robot-assisted laparoscopic radical cystoprostatectomy with bilateral extended pelvic lymphadenectomy and ileal conduit urinary diversion.

The patient's diagnosis was bladder cancer, and he was prescribed medications at discharge. His physical exam was normal, with no abnormalities noted.

The patient's laboratory results showed normal blood counts, with some fluctuations in his white blood cell count and hemoglobin levels. His blood glucose levels were also monitored, and he had some fluctuations in his levels. His calcium and phosphorus levels were also monitored, and he had some fluctuations in his levels.

The patient's hospital course was uneventful, with no acute cardiopulmonary processes noted. He was started on strict sips on POD 3 and advanced to a regular diet on POD 4. He received one dose of unasyn and his nephrostomy tube was clamped on POD 5.

In summary, the patient is a 58-year-old male who underwent a robot-assisted laparoscopic radical cystoprostatectomy with bilateral extended pelvic lymphadenectomy and ileal conduit urinary diversion due to bladder cancer. His past medical history includes several conditions, and he has allergies to adhesive tape and Dicloxacillin. His laboratory results showed some fluctuations in his blood counts and blood glucose levels, but no acute cardiopulmonary processes were noted. He was started on strict sips on POD 3 and advanced to a regular diet on POD 4.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: This 29-year-old female with a history of progressive lower back pain and left leg weakness was admitted for surgical management of severe spinal stenosis and a 1 cm L5-S1 disc protrusion. She underwent an uncomplicated left L5-S1 discectomy and demonstrated a smooth post-operative recovery, achieving independent ambulation and good pain control with oral medications. At the time of discharge, she remained neurologically stable with only trace residual weakness in left plantar and dorsiflexion and slight sensory loss in her left foot. She was discharged home in stable condition with a medication regimen including gabapentin, citalopram, docusate, senna, and as-needed analgesics. Her discharge instructions focused on wound care and activity modifications, specifically avoiding lifting more than 10 pounds or excessive bending and twisting for a period of two weeks.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis: The patient is a 29-year-old female who was admitted to the neurosurgery service with a chief complaint of leg pain and weakness. She had a history of lower back pain (LBP) that began one month prior, which worsened while on vacation and led to unbearable pain and weakness in her left calf. She was treated conservatively with ESI and medications, but her pain persisted. The patient underwent a left L5-S1 discectomy and was diagnosed with a marked severe L5-S1 discogenic disease with spinal canal stenosis, neural foraminal narrowing, and nerve root impingement. She was prescribed several medications, including docusate sodium, senna, acetaminophen, ibuprofen, oxycodone, gabapentin, and citalopram. The patient was discharged home with instructions to keep her wound clean, avoid heavy lifting, and take pain medication as instructed.

scenario_results.doctor_diagnosis: This 53-year-old female with primary sclerosing cholangitis and cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B, MELD 11) was admitted for right upper quadrant abdominal pain and fever concerning for recurrent cholangitis. Diagnostic imaging with MRCP and ultrasound demonstrated biliary sludge and a stone in the gallbladder remnant, which was addressed via an ERCP that removed scant sludge from the biliary tree. Given her history of multi-drug resistant infections, her antibiotic therapy was transitioned to Meropenem 1g every 8 hours via a PICC line, to be completed seven days post-procedure. An EGD performed during the admission identified an 8 mm gastroesophageal junction ulcer and portal gastropathy without evidence of active varices. Throughout the hospitalization, the patient remained neurologically intact with no signs of hepatic encephalopathy, and her chronic medications—including rifaximin, lactulose, and ursodiol—were continued. Her home diuretics were held on admission but restarted prior to discharge once her fluid status was stabilized. At the time of discharge, the patient was afebrile, ambulatory, and independent. She was instructed to complete her outpatient IV antibiotic course, continue a gluten-free diet, and follow up for scheduled laboratory monitoring and hepatology clinic appointments.
scenario_results.correct_diagnosis:
The patient is a 53-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital with abdominal pain and fever. She has a history of primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), cirrhosis, ascites, hepatic encephalopathy, varices, and possibly Crohn's disease and celiac disease. She was diagnosed with cholangitis during a previous hospitalization and was treated with IV antibiotics. She has also had a laparoscopic cholecystectomy and liver biopsy, which showed bile ductular proliferation and early, incomplete nodule formation.

The patient's allergies include Bactrim, Ampicillin, Sulfa, cefuroxime, Gadolinium-Containing Contrast Media, gluten, nadolol, and Pneumovax 23. She has a history of hypertension, GERD, hypothyroidism, and OSA.

During her hospitalization, the patient's symptoms included abdominal pain, fever, nausea, and vomiting. She was treated with ertapenem and Zofran, and her nausea improved with the latter. Her laboratory results showed slightly elevated liver enzymes, but her chemistry panel and urinalysis were normal.

The patient underwent an ERCP procedure, which showed no dominant stricture in the CBD or CHD, but did show marked narrowing of the right and left common hepatic ducts consistent with known PSC. She also underwent an MRCP procedure, which showed cirrhosis complicated by portal hypertension, splenomegaly, and multiple esophageal and gastric varices.

At discharge, the patient was prescribed medications including ertapenem and Zofran, and was advised to follow up with her primary care physician.
